title: podman: set umask to 022
body:
having an umask other than 0 might prevent us for creating directories
with mode 0755.  Set the umask very early in the program startup.

Closes: https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/2074

Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Scrivano <gscrivan@redhat.com>

comments.body:
This does not seem like a good idea?  

```
2 Answers. Setting the umask to 0000 (or just 0 ) means that newly created files or directories created will have no privileges initially revoked. In other words, a umask of zero will cause all files to be created as 0666 or world-writable. Directories created while umask is 0 will be 0777 .
```

If you wipe out all of the files in your home dir, what do is the permissions on the files and directories?  Similar what happens in /var/run/containers and /var/lib/containers?

it won't make any difference on the files/directories created by podman.  What `umask` does, is to drop some bits from the permission mask.
For example, an umask set to `077` as in the original issue, will prevent any created file from being accessible from users other than root (the bits in the umask are turned off from the specified permissions).

With umask = 0:

os.MkdirAll(root, 0755 & ~umask) => os.MkdirAll(root, 0755 & ~0) =>  => os.MkdirAll(root, 0755)

With umask = 077:

os.MkdirAll(root, 0755 & ~umask) => os.MkdirAll(root, 0755 & ~077) =>  => os.MkdirAll(root, 0700)

On a fresh created storage:

```
# stat -c%+a /var/lib/containers
700
# stat -c%+a /var/lib/containers/storage
700
# stat -c%+a /var/run/containers
700
```
I don't think we actually want to make any world-writable folders/files, so should we set to 022 instead?
@giuseppe But what happens when we create a file without specifying the umask?

> I don't think we actually want to make any world-writable folders/files, so should we set to 022 instead?

sure, that should work for now.  Updated

> @giuseppe But what happens when we create a file without specifying the umask?

that is not possible.  An umask is always specified when creating a new file.  The closest is to forget to specify it to open(2) with `O_CREAT`, but that is a bug in the program and AFAICS we are not creating any file from the C code.

@giuseppe Does not prevent us from future issues, though when someone creates a file or directory without knowing this is set, and we end up creating content as 666 or 777.

I am a lot less worried with 0022.
LGTM
/lgtm

Today I tried to use [this `zola` container](https://hub.docker.com/r/j1mc/docker-zola):

```
podman run --rm -w /work -v $(PWD):/work:Z -p 1111:1111 j1mc/docker-zola build
```

…and the command it ran created a directory with permissions 0777. I was under the impression that this PR would result in a directory with 0755 permissions, or at 0775 (my shell process's umask is 0002).

Am I missing something here…? :thinking:

are you using crun?

I've fixed it recently: https://github.com/containers/crun/pull/261
